assumptions: A declared intragroup dividend recognised as a receivable/payable is treated as an intragroup monetary item.
The entities involved have different functional currencies; otherwise there is no relevant foreign currency risk.
The question concerns eligibility of the hedged item in consolidated financial statements, not the full accounting for the hedging instrument.
No facts indicate that the dividend balance is being analysed as part of a net investment in a foreign operation.

approaches.label: General intragroup hedged-item prohibition
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, hedged items generally must be with a party external to the reporting entity. An ordinary intragroup item is therefore not eligible unless a specific exception applies.
approaches.practical_implication: Start from a presumption of ineligibility for intragroup balances in consolidation.

source_contributions.summary: IFRS 9.6.3.5 limits hedged items in consolidation to assets, liabilities, firm commitments or highly probable forecast transactions with external parties, subject to stated exceptions.

approaches.label: Foreign currency hedge of an intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 expressly allows the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to be a hedged item in consolidated financial statements when resulting exchange gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
conditions: The dividend balance is a recognised intragroup monetary receivable/payable
The counterparties have different functional currencies
The foreign currency gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
The hedge relationship is formally designated and documented at inception
The IFRS 9 hedge effectiveness criteria are met
approaches.practical_implication: A recognised intragroup dividend receivable may be hedge-designated in consolidation if its FX exposure survives consolidation.

source_contributions.summary: IFRS 9.6.3.6 creates a specific exception for foreign currency risk on intragroup monetary items that produces exchange differences not fully eliminated on consolidation.
IFRS 9.6.4.1 requires formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness for any qualifying hedge relationship.

approaches.label: Cash flow hedge of a forecast intragroup transaction
approaches.reasoning: A highly probable forecast intragroup transaction can qualify in consolidation only if it is denominated in a currency other than the functional currency of the transacting entity and its FX risk will affect consolidated profit or loss.
conditions: The item is still a forecast transaction, not yet a recognised receivable
It is denominated in a currency other than the functional currency of the entity entering into it
Its foreign currency risk will affect consolidated profit or loss
The hedge relationship is formally designated and documented at inception
approaches.practical_implication: This model is relevant only before recognition; once the dividend is booked as a receivable, the monetary-item exception is the more relevant lens.

source_contributions.summary: IFRS 9.6.3.6 allows certain forecast intragroup transactions to be hedged in consolidated financial statements.
IFRS 9.B6.3.5-B6.3.6 requires that the FX risk of the forecast intragroup transaction affect consolidated profit or loss and explains the OCI recycling consequence.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge in a foreign operation
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 addresses hedges of the foreign currency risk of a net investment in a foreign operation, where the hedged item is an amount of net assets. That is a different hedge model from hedging a declared intragroup dividend receivable.
conditions: Relevant only if the hedged item is an amount of net assets of a foreign operation
approaches.practical_implication: Do not analyse an ordinary intragroup dividend receivable as a net investment hedge unless the hedged exposure is the net investment itself.

source_contributions.summary: IFRIC 16.10-14 frames the hedged risk and hedging instrument rules for net investment hedges, with the hedged item being net assets of the foreign operation.
IFRIC 16.13 states that the same foreign currency exposure from a net investment can qualify for hedge accounting only once in the consolidated financial statements.
